header.latest_update_cutoff: U.S. fixed-income early close · 2:00 p.m. ET
header.stance: Front-end relief steepened the curve while long-duration pressure held into the 2:00 p.m. early close
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 2 Treasury close interrupted the real-yield-led selloff of the previous two sessions without delivering a broad duration rally. Softer US employment data pulled policy-sensitive yields lower and pushed an immediate Federal Reserve increase further from the center of the market’s distribution. The long end did not follow. Benchmark and long-bond yields held slightly above the prior day, leaving the curve steeper and preserving the term-premium pressure that had dominated July 1. This was a SIFMA-recommended early close at 2:00 p.m. ET, so the outcome is best understood as a front-end policy repricing inside a still-demanding long-duration environment, not as a regular full-session verdict.

The central interpretation therefore changed in one important respect. July 1 had combined softer inflation signals with rising real yields across the curve, implying that growth resilience, uncertain Fed communication and the long-end clearing rate outweighed disinflation. July 2 weakened the growth side of that argument. June payroll growth slowed sharply, earlier months were revised down, participation fell and factory orders contracted. Initial claims remained low, however, and shipments and backlogs still expanded. The evidence reduced the urgency of a near-term hike without establishing a rapid labor or demand break.

Three transmission channels defined the shortened close. The labor report and lower oil shifted Fed pricing and favored the two-year sector. Stable breakevens and positive long real-yield changes kept the benchmark and long end from sharing that rally. Global duration supplied an additional restraint: a weak Japanese government-bond auction, persistent yen pressure and concern that the BOJ could fall behind inflation pushed Japanese yields sharply higher, sustaining a cross-market term-premium hurdle even as the ECB debate became less urgent.

Credit and cross assets made the message less defensive than the rates curve alone might suggest. Listed corporate and emerging-market debt gauges later finished modestly higher, yet the high-grade market’s growing concentration in AI-related borrowers raised a separate absorption risk beneath tight aggregate pricing. The dollar weakened and gold rose after the fixed-income cutoff, reinforcing the softer near-term Fed interpretation. A deep semiconductor selloff alongside subdued volatility showed that risk aversion was concentrated, not systemic. The close thus reversed immediate hike anxiety while leaving long-duration compensation, primary-market concentration and global policy dispersion unresolved.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury’s official daily reference series, timestamped 5:00 p.m. ET, recorded the two-year yield at 4.14%, down three basis points, and the five-year at 4.23%, down one. The 10-year rose one basis point to 4.49%, and the 30-year added one to 4.98%. These are daily references for the shortened session, not exact observations at the SIFMA-recommended early close at 2:00 p.m. ET. They nevertheless capture the defining split: policy-sensitive maturities rallied after the employment release, while benchmark and long duration retained the prior session’s elevated clearing rate.

Curve slopes translated that divergence cleanly. The 2s10s spread widened four basis points to 35, and 2s30s widened four to 84. The 2s5s10s butterfly was unchanged at minus 17, showing no fresh dislocation in the five-year against its wings. The 2s10s30s butterfly rose four basis points to minus 14 as the 10-year cheapened relative to the average of the two-year and long bond. The move was therefore a policy-led bull steepening at the front, with no corresponding fall in longer yields.

The broader horizon prevented the front-end rally from becoming a regime change. Over one week, two-, five-, 10- and 30-year yields were still higher by five, eight, nine and 12 basis points. Over one month, the two-year remained nine basis points higher and the five-year five higher, whereas the 10-year was only two higher and the 30-year one lower. July 2 repaired part of the immediate policy shock but did not erase the week’s synchronized cheapening or the longer-running difference between front-end Fed risk and far-end duration demand.

June employment supplied the main causal break before the cutoff. Nonfarm payrolls increased by 57,000, below the 113,000 median estimate, after downward revisions to the previous two months. Unemployment fell to 4.2%, but the decline reflected a sharp drop in labor-force participation, limiting its hawkish content. Short-rate futures reduced the probability of a July 29 increase to about 20% from roughly 33% before the release. A quarter-point move remained fully priced for the year, though the expected timing shifted to December from October.

The accompanying releases kept the repricing measured. Initial claims fell by 1,000 to 215,000 in the week ended June 27, and their four-week average declined to 222,000. Insured unemployment rose by 2,000 to 1.814 million, with its four-week average increasing to 1.803 million. The flow of new layoffs remained contained even as continued claims suggested slower re-employment. May factory orders fell 1.3% after April’s 5.3% increase, yet shipments rose 1.6%, unfilled orders gained 0.6% and inventories increased 0.2%. Demand cooled, but production pipelines did not collapse.

Inflation-linked pricing explains why longer nominal yields resisted the labor relief. The 10-year real yield rose one basis point to 2.26%, while the 10-year breakeven was unchanged at 2.23%. Five-year real yields added one basis point to 1.99%; seven-year real yields held at 2.11%; and 20- and 30-year real yields rose two and one basis points to 2.60% and 2.79%. The benchmark nominal increase came from real compensation, not a renewed rise in inflation expectations. Cheaper oil helped restrain breakevens, but it did not reduce the real return required for intermediate and long duration.

Cash bills were more uneven than the coupon curve. Four-week coupon-equivalent yields rose four basis points to 3.67%, and eight-week yields added three to 3.72%. Six-week yields fell three basis points to 3.67%, while 13-, 17-, 26- and 52-week yields declined by three to four basis points, ending between 3.76% and 3.95%. The pattern concentrated pressure in the maturities sold during the shortened session and did not resemble a uniform holiday funding squeeze.

The 28-day auction accepted $85.0001367 billion at a 3.605% high rate with 2.72 bid-to-cover. Indirect bidders took 67.05% of competitive awards, direct bidders 3.75% and dealers 29.20%; 46.77% was allotted at the high. The 56-day sale accepted $85.0007166 billion at 3.650% with 2.70 coverage. Its indirect, direct and dealer shares were 61.72%, 3.90% and 34.38%, and 53.99% was allotted at the high. Both securities issue and settle July 7, so the bidding outcome established pre-holiday absorption while the cash transfer remains a later event.

The shortened close consequently separated two questions that had moved together earlier in the week. Payrolls and participation reduced the need for an imminent Fed response, and pre-release short positioning likely amplified the front-end rally. Long maturities still faced positive real yields, upcoming coupon supply and an unsettled global term premium. Equity, currency, commodity and volatility closes recorded at 5:00 p.m. ET can confirm or complicate that interpretation only as later evidence; they did not cause the completed fixed-income move after the 2:00 p.m. ET cutoff.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Broad listed credit tone was resilient after the rates close. At the 5:00 p.m. ET equity-market reference, LQD gained 0.1660%, HYG rose 0.1508% and JNK added 0.1147%. The modest, similar direction across quality tiers did not indicate a broad risk liquidation despite the semiconductor shock. The more consequential credit signal came from the changing composition of high-grade supply and the capacity required to absorb it, not from a large move in listed funds.

High-rated technology issuance has mechanically improved index quality while increasing concentration. AA- and A-rated bonds represented 52% of the US investment-grade index at the end of June, up from about 46% in 2021, as BBB exposure fell to 41% from 46%. High-grade spreads were only 0.74 percentage point on July 1, near multi-decade lows. Strong current ratings therefore supported tight aggregate pricing, but they also made the index more dependent on a narrow group of cash-rich technology borrowers.

The supply scale sharpened that dependence. Companies had sold about $220 billion of AI-linked bonds across currencies during 2026, 62% more than in all of 2025. Four hyperscalers—Amazon, Meta, Alphabet and Oracle—accounted for roughly $107 billion of dollar issuance. Nvidia and SpaceX each sold $25 billion in June, helping monthly high-grade volume reach a record. The six largest US hyperscalers were preparing nearly $820 billion of AI spending during 2026, suggesting that primary-market absorption remains a recurring requirement rather than a completed funding event.

Balance-sheet strength lowers immediate default risk but does not remove maturity or return-on-investment risk. Longer bonds can outlive the economic usefulness of the assets they finance: Alphabet issued sterling debt with a 100-year maturity, and Nvidia and SpaceX included 20- and 30-year tranches. SpaceX’s debut bonds weakened rapidly in secondary trading, showing that ratings and headline demand do not guarantee stable post-pricing performance. Continued issuance could widen spreads through supply even if underlying cash flows remain sound; disappointing AI returns would add leverage and refinancing pressure.

Japan offered a different form of primary-market adaptation. Companies sold ¥1 trillion of convertible bonds in the first half, the most for that period since 2004. Nippon Steel’s ¥600 billion transaction was the largest Japanese convertible issue on record, and JX Advanced Metals raised ¥250 billion. Many deals carried zero coupons, allowing issuers to reduce cash interest costs while higher equity valuations supplied conversion value. Rising domestic policy rates made that hybrid channel more attractive, though its economics depend on equity performance as well as creditor protection.

Pre-holiday bill auctions showed that cash investors could absorb substantial government supply before the interruption, but their July 7 settlement leaves a later funding test. The Federal Reserve’s H.4.1 release at 4:30 p.m. ET supplied subsequent context only: reserve balances averaged $2.967 trillion in the week ended July 1, up $15.5 billion, as the Treasury General Account fell $38.5 billion. That later liquidity snapshot cannot explain the earlier close. It does show that reserves had risen going into the next settlement cycle, alongside higher reverse repos and a smaller securities portfolio.

Credit access thus remained open across public bonds, convertibles and listed risk vehicles, with material differentiation in what investors were financing. High current ratings and large issuer cash flows supported absorption; tight spreads, long asset lives and repeated AI supply reduced the margin for disappointment. The key fault line was concentration and structure, not a same-day shutdown in funding.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

European data extended the disinflation impulse that had failed to rally Treasuries on July 1. Euro-area inflation slowed to 2.8% in June from 3.2% in May and came in below a 3.0% estimate. May unemployment held at 6.2%, with the number of unemployed people down 55,000 from April and 158,000 from a year earlier. First-quarter house prices still rose 4.7% from a year earlier, although that pace eased from 5.1% in the fourth quarter. The combination reduced immediate price pressure without presenting a weak labor or household-balance-sheet backdrop.

ECB communication preserved a two-sided policy consequence. Christine Lagarde defended June’s increase because the energy shock had been spreading through the economy when the decision was made. She also acknowledged that second-round effects had not materialized. Philip Lane remained attentive to delayed pass-through into wages, food and services, whereas Olli Rehn and Martin Kocher judged the near-term threat to have diminished after oil returned toward prewar levels. European front-end rates therefore faced less urgency, but the Governing Council had not declared the inflation shock resolved.

The UK supplied a more explicit upside-policy tail. Bank of England policymaker Catherine Mann supported holding the policy rate at 3.75% for now, yet said persistent inflation expectations, energy costs, profit-margin behavior or adverse wage negotiations could warrant an activist increase. Her stance followed a 7-2 vote against a quarter-point hike at the prior meeting. The direct transmission to global duration is a higher conditional floor for sterling rates if expectations drift, even as current oil relief reduces the need for immediate action.

Japan moved decisively in the other direction. A benchmark 10-year JGB auction produced a 3.13 bid-to-cover ratio, down from 3.53 at the previous sale and below the 3.33 one-year average. The 10-year yield rose seven basis points to 2.77%, and the 20-year climbed 9.5 basis points to 3.76%. Demand weakened as investors weighed fiscal expansion and the risk that gradual BOJ tightening would allow inflation pressure to persist. The auction made the long-end concern observable through bidding and secondary yields, not merely through policy commentary.

Currency, policy and buyer-base evidence reinforced that pressure. The BOJ had raised its benchmark rate to 1%, the highest since 1995, yet markets assigned more than a 60% probability to another increase by October as the yen remained weak. Firms’ longer-term inflation expectations in the Tankan reached 2.6%, the highest in the series beginning in 2014. Overseas investors sold ¥3.12 trillion of Japanese bonds during June, the largest outflow in three years, as BOJ purchases receded and government supply rose. Those channels increase the domestic yield needed to retain buyers.

Fiscal expectations added a separate premium. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi targeted an expansion of nominal GDP to $6.8 trillion in the fiscal year beginning April 2040, and investors awaited details on a possible suspension of the sales tax on food and beverages. The policy may support nominal activity, but it also raises questions about issuance and inflation while the central bank normalizes cautiously. A higher JGB clearing rate can reduce the relative appeal of foreign duration for Japanese investors, transmitting pressure into other sovereign long ends even without a synchronized inflation shock.

Australia contributed a smaller regional growth counterweight. Its May goods balance swung to an A$3.018 billion deficit from a A$1.383 billion surplus as exports fell 6.9% and imports rose 2.6%. A 35% decline in non-monetary-gold exports drove most of the export weakness. The release occurred at 9:30 p.m. ET on July 1, before the US shortened session, and pointed to a softer external-demand impulse. That signal could restrain Australian duration, but it was too narrow to offset the stronger Japanese term-premium channel.

Global duration therefore became more dispersed, not uniformly easier. Euro-area disinflation reduced the probability of rapid follow-up tightening, and Australian trade softened. Japan combined weak auction demand, foreign selling, fiscal ambition and currency-linked inflation risk, while the BOE kept a conditional hike threat alive. The US long end’s refusal to rally after soft payrolls fits that international backdrop: lower near-term policy urgency can coexist with a high real and term-premium hurdle when sovereign supply and buyer incentives are moving in different directions.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market financial conditions improved at the margin after the US labor release. Major EM currencies rose as investors reduced the urgency of Fed tightening, with the South African rand and Hungarian forint gaining about 1% and several Latin American currencies advancing. The Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index was down 0.5% during the US session. The mechanism was clearest in currencies: a less immediate Fed hike lowers the relative-rate hurdle and eases the cost of servicing dollar liabilities.

Local-currency sovereign demand had already shown resilience before the US repricing. Foreign inflows into government bonds in Thailand, Indonesia, India and Malaysia reached $8.2 billion in June, the strongest combined total in more than two years. Expectations that regional central banks would keep rates elevated supported carry even when investors feared renewed Fed tightening. Since the US-Iran agreement, an emerging-Asia bond index had gained 0.2%, compared with a 0.6% decline in a Latin American gauge, demonstrating that energy exposure and domestic policy still produced regional dispersion.

The same resilience contains a constraint. Elevated local rates can attract foreign capital, but they also reflect persistent inflation and tighter domestic financing conditions. South Korean consumer inflation accelerated to 3.2% in June from 3.1%, while core inflation held at 2.5%. Strong exports, a weak currency and housing pressure kept the Bank of Korea’s bias restrictive. For sovereign curves, foreign inflows can support absorption only while domestic credibility and currency stability compensate investors for the rate risk.

Later-closing dollar debt provided mild confirmation, with EMB gaining 0.1875% at the 5:00 p.m. ET reference. That total return combined Treasury duration and sovereign risk, so country-level funding conditions remained more informative than the aggregate move. A softer dollar aided hard-currency borrowers and commodity importers, but it did not settle refinancing risk for issuers with short maturities, weak reserves or dependence on foreign inflows.

Turkey and Colombia remained examples of that vulnerability because both rely more heavily on external capital and would face a sharper tightening if the Fed returned to a hawkish path. Oil’s large monthly decline helped importers’ trade and inflation arithmetic, while reduced energy revenue worked against exporters. July 2 eased the common dollar-liquidity pressure; it did not produce a uniform sovereign outcome. Local-currency debt, hard-currency bonds and corporate funding continued to respond to different combinations of policy credibility, reserve capacity and terms of trade.

## Cross asset

Cross assets recorded at the 5:00 p.m. ET New York reference mostly reinforced the front-end policy repricing after fixed income had already closed. The technology selloff was underway before 2:00 p.m., but final equity, volatility, currency and commodity values are later evidence. They can show whether the rates interpretation persisted through the rest of the day; they cannot reverse the causal order of the shortened Treasury session.

Equity performance exposed sharp internal dispersion. The S&P 500 ended essentially unchanged at 7,483.24, while the Nasdaq 100 fell 1.61% and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index dropped 5.4448%. The VIX declined 2.6522% to 16.15 despite those losses. Broad stability and lower implied volatility argued against a systemic growth shock, while the chip rout challenged confidence in the sector carrying a large share of investment-grade borrowing and capital expenditure.

The dollar and gold aligned more directly with reduced near-term Fed pressure. The Dollar Index fell 0.5227% to 100.86, and gold rose 1.0914% to $4,112.70. A weaker dollar eased global funding conditions and matched the decline in immediate hike probability. Gold’s gain also reflected lower yields and renewed demand for a non-yielding asset, though it did not signal a generalized haven episode when broad equities were flat and volatility fell.

Energy preserved the disinflationary medium-term context without adding a fresh daily impulse. WTI settled at $68.69, up 0.1604%, and Brent at $71.80, up 0.3214%. Over one month they were lower by 25.4666% and 24.4051%. The near-flat daily closes explain why 10-year breakevens held steady after their recent retreat. The much larger monthly decline remained central to the reduction in inflation anxiety cited by Fed and ECB officials.

Taken together, later markets confirmed a narrower interpretation of the Treasury move. Dollar weakness and gold strength supported less urgent Fed tightening; flat broad equities and falling volatility denied the labor miss a recessionary reading. Semiconductor losses complicated the growth story and highlighted credit concentration, yet they did not generate a broad haven rally. The unresolved cross-asset question is whether the AI correction stays sector-specific or begins to weaken investment, issuance and risk appetite enough to pull long real yields lower.

## Forward Market Significance

The first post-holiday US macro sequence begins July 6 at 10:00 a.m. ET with ISM services and the New York Fed Global Supply Chain Pressure Index, followed July 7 by May trade at 8:30 a.m. and the June Survey of Consumer Expectations at 11:00. The June 16-17 FOMC minutes arrive July 8 at 2:00 p.m. A softer services-employment and prices mix, contained logistics costs and easing household inflation expectations would confirm that the payroll slowdown can keep July policy unchanged, with the strongest response in two- to five-year yields. Resilient services demand, renewed price pressure or minutes showing broad support for a 2026 increase would challenge that front-end relief and restore the restrictive reaction function that drove the prior week’s selloff.

Treasury absorption becomes a separate test from July 6 through July 9. The July 6 sales comprise $92 billion of 13-week bills and $79 billion of 26-week bills at 11:30 a.m. ET; July 7 brings $90 billion of 42-day bills, $52 billion of 52-week bills and a $58 billion three-year note. Reopenings of $39 billion in 10-year notes on July 8 and $22 billion in 30-year bonds on July 9 complete the sequence. Broad bidding, contained dealer awards and orderly July 9 bill and July 15 coupon settlements would resolve the immediate holiday-funding question while supporting the current curve split. Weak participation or persistent secondary cheapening would reopen balance-sheet concerns and transmit from cash bills into front-end liquidity or from coupon supply into the long-end term premium.

The global-duration divergence faces its clearest test in Japan and the euro area. Japan’s roughly ¥3.8 trillion three-month-bill auction is due July 3, its approximately ¥600 billion 30-year JGB sale July 7 and its roughly ¥2.5 trillion five-year auction July 9; euro-area producer prices and retail sales arrive July 6 at 5:00 a.m. ET. Strong Japanese end-investor demand alongside softer European pipeline prices and consumption would challenge the persistence of July 2’s global term-premium restraint and allow US long yields to respond more fully to weaker labor. Another weak JGB auction, renewed yen pressure or firm euro-area prices would confirm policy and buyer-base dispersion, keeping the long end sensitive to real-yield and supply compensation even if the Fed remains on hold.

section_summaries.market_read: The close removed an immediate policy shock but left the long-end clearing hurdle intact.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether weaker growth evidence finally pulls long real yields lower after the holiday.
section_summaries.summary: July 2 reversed immediate hike anxiety through softer labor data and lower oil, producing a front-end-led steepening rather than a broad duration rally. Stable inflation compensation, elevated real yields, Japanese term-premium pressure and concentrated AI financing needs kept longer-maturity and credit risks unresolved.

section_summaries.market_read: Fed urgency eased at the front end, while real-yield demand preserved a steep-curve bias.
section_summaries.next_check: Services prices and employment will show whether front-end relief can extend beyond payrolls.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Payroll disappointment and weaker participation shifted Fed pricing toward a later move, driving a policy-led bull steepening without easing long-end real compensation. Contained layoffs, intact production pipelines and positive weekly yield changes kept the move measured and short of a regime shift.

section_summaries.market_read: Open funding channels coexist with a concentration premium that aggregate pricing understates.
section_summaries.next_check: Track secondary performance and dealer absorption as AI-linked issuance continues.
section_summaries.summary: Listed debt gauges later showed resilient broad risk tone, but tight high-grade pricing masks rising dependence on a narrow group of AI borrowers. Strong ratings and cash flows support access, while repeated long-dated supply and uncertain project returns reduce the margin for post-issuance disappointment.

section_summaries.market_read: Global easing signals no longer guarantee lower long yields when buyer demand and supply diverge.
section_summaries.next_check: Japan’s next auctions will test whether weak end demand keeps pressure on sovereign term premia.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Euro-area disinflation reduced near-term tightening urgency, but Japan’s weak auction demand, foreign selling, fiscal ambition and yen-linked inflation pressure raised the global duration hurdle. The result is policy dispersion: softer European conditions can coexist with a high clearing rate for US and Japanese long bonds.

section_summaries.market_read: Dollar relief improved EM liquidity at the margin, but domestic credibility still governs absorption.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether Fed relief persists without currency weakness forcing restrictive local policy.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A softer dollar and lower near-term Fed urgency eased the common financing hurdle for emerging markets, supporting currencies and later listed debt tone. Yet restrictive local policy, uneven energy exposure and reliance on foreign inflows preserved country-level dispersion and left vulnerable borrowers sensitive to a hawkish reversal.

section_summaries.market_read: Later cross assets confirmed policy relief but challenged the AI-led growth and funding story.
section_summaries.next_check: The key test is whether semiconductor weakness spreads into investment, issuance or broad risk.
section_summaries.summary: After fixed income closed, dollar weakness and gold strength supported the reduced-hike interpretation, while flat broad equities and lower volatility denied a systemic growth shock. The semiconductor rout instead concentrated concern around AI investment, issuance and the durability of long real-yield pressure.

section_summaries.market_read: The curve needs both softer policy inputs and credible long-duration absorption to rally broadly.
section_summaries.next_check: Follow the July 6-9 data and auction sequence for a break in the front-end/long-end divide.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests will decide whether front-end relief can broaden: the post-holiday US macro sequence, Treasury’s bill and coupon supply, and Japanese auctions alongside euro-area data. Softer activity and orderly bidding would validate the split; renewed prices pressure or weak duration demand would restore tightening and term-premium risk.

methodology_note: U.S. fixed-income rows are same-date official daily references for the SIFMA-recommended session ending at 2:00 p.m. ET; their source timestamps are preserved. Later-closing cross-asset rows appear only when same-date data is available.

UST 2s10s curve 35 bp (+4 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 84 bp (+4 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Soft payrolls lift front-end Treasuries, but real-yield pressure holds the long end
overall_summary.session_narrative: By the SIFMA-recommended 2:00 p.m. ET close, weaker payroll growth, downward revisions and lower participation had shifted Fed pricing toward a later move, pulling the front end lower. Stable breakevens, positive long real-yield changes and weak Japanese duration demand limited the rally beyond five years. Later dollar weakness, gold strength and resilient listed credit confirmed the narrower policy-relief reading, while the semiconductor selloff remained concentrated rather than systemic.
overall_summary.summary: Softer employment data reduced the urgency of a near-term Fed increase and supported policy-sensitive Treasuries. Longer maturities resisted as real yields, supply needs and global term-premium pressure remained firm, leaving the early-close curve steeper rather than delivering a broad duration rally.

watch_events.market_implication: Softness would extend relief in two- to five-year yields; resilience would restore hike risk.
watch_events.market_trigger: Soft employment and prices with easing expectations, or renewed demand and broad hike support.
watch_events.title: Post-holiday US macro and Fed test
watch_events.watch_line: Track services hiring and prices, supply-chain costs, household expectations and FOMC tone.

watch_events.market_implication: Strong absorption preserves the curve split; weakness raises funding or long-end term-premium pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad demand and orderly settlement, or weak participation with persistent secondary cheapening.
watch_events.title: Treasury supply and settlement test
watch_events.watch_line: Watch bidding breadth, dealer awards and secondary performance through bill and coupon sales.

watch_events.market_implication: Improvement would free US duration to rally; renewed dispersion would keep long yields pressured.
watch_events.market_trigger: Strong JGB bidding with softer European data, or another weak auction and firm prices.
watch_events.title: Japan and euro-area duration test
watch_events.watch_line: Gauge Japanese end demand, yen pressure, and euro-area pipeline prices and consumption.
